story.logline: A handmade glass earns its value through one precise pour.
story.dramaticQuestion: Can a simple material action feel ceremonial without becoming artificial?

beats.title: Anticipation
beats.purpose: Establish the glass and empty space.

scenes.title: Workshop Table
scenes.purpose: Make the product feel physically real and considered.

shots.title: The Pour
shots.intent: Turn material detail into the product argument.
shots.subject: a hand-blown clear tumbler on a scarred walnut workbench
shots.action: amber liquid enters the glass, curls around the base, rises, and settles
shots.environment: a working glass studio with a distant furnace glow

camera.framing: glass centered low with negative space above the rim
camera.focusBehavior: focus holds on the front rim then transfers to the liquid surface

lighting.motivation: late-afternoon daylight with furnace warmth from frame right

physics: liquid obeys gravity and glass-wall adhesion
surface settles with diminishing concentric waves
glass remains fixed to the workbench

beats.action: hold on the empty glass while furnace reflections move faintly
the pour enters from above frame and visibly fills the lower two-thirds
the stream stops and the liquid surface settles into a clean final composition

continuityLocks: same tumbler geometry and rim thickness
same workbench scratches and glass position
imperfectionAnchors: tiny trapped bubbles in the hand-blown glass
fine dust visible only in the backlight
subtle edge halation at the furnace reflection

soundDesignDirectives: close liquid pour with a detailed glass resonance
quiet workshop room tone and distant furnace breath
